narration_segments.text: The breath arrives before the thought. Always. Every single time. But the anxious mind reverses this order. It insists that the thought came first. It insists the spiral started somewhere in language, somewhere in logic, somewhere a person could trace and fix and prevent. Joshin sits with this misunderstanding every morning. He sits on a round cushion in a room with one window. The window faces east. The light enters slowly. And the first thing Joshin notices — before his knees settle, before his spine finds its line — is that the breath is already here.

Kenji is thirty-four. He designs websites for small businesses in Osaka. He is good at his work. His clients like him. His reviews are strong. His portfolio is clean and modern and reflects the careful attention he brings to every project. None of this information stops the feeling that arrives every Sunday evening at approximately seven o'clock. It starts in his stomach. A tightening. Not pain — tightening. Like someone slowly turning a dial. His breathing shifts. It moves from his belly to his upper chest. The breaths grow shorter, faster, shallower. His thoughts begin to accelerate. Not with new information. With old information dressed in new urgency. Monday. The client revision. The email he has not answered. The meeting he does not want to attend. The invoice he forgot to send. The possibility — vivid, specific, and entirely fictional — that he has made a catastrophic error in the website code he delivered on Friday. He has not made an error. He checked the code three times. He ran the browser tests. He previewed every page on three devices. But the feeling does not consult the evidence.

This chapter is a practice. The listener does it now. Not later. Not when conditions improve. Not when the room is quieter or the mind is calmer or the day is less demanding. Now. The conditions are already adequate. Zazen does not require perfect conditions. It requires a body and a willingness to remain still. Find a place to sit. A chair works. The floor works. A park bench works. A train seat works. The surface does not matter. The posture does: upright without rigid. A straight spine. Not military straight — alive straight. The kind of straight that a plant grows toward light. Effortful at the root, easy at the top. Feet flat on the ground if in a chair. Crossed legs if on the floor. Hands resting on the thighs, palms down. This is not decoration. Palms down settles the nervous system.

The listener returns to the day now. The practice ends. The mirror stays. Joshin does not ask anyone to meditate for hours. He does not ask anyone to retreat from the world, to become calm, to transcend anxiety, to achieve a state of permanent peace. He finds these goals misguided. They treat the human condition as a problem to be solved rather than a reality to be inhabited. Anxiety is not a disease to be cured. It is a signal to be understood. The nervous system speaks through sensation. It says: something requires attention. Something shifted in the environment.
